# Practical Applications of Self-Healing Polymers Beyond Mechanical and Electrical Recovery

### Affiliations

# Practical Applications of Self-Healing Polymers Beyond Mechanical and Electrical Recovery

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Self-healing polymeric materials, which can repair physical damage, offer promising prospects for protective applications across various industries. Although prolonged durability and resource conservation are key advantages, focusing solely on mechanical recovery may limit the market potential of these materials. The unique physical properties of self-healing polymers, such as interfacial reduction, seamless connection lines, temperature/pressure responses, and phase transitions, enable a multitude of innovative applications. In this perspective, the diverse applications of self-healing polymers beyond their traditional mechanical strength are emphasized and their potential in various sectors such as food packaging, damage-reporting, radiation shielding, acoustic conservation, biomedical monitoring, and tissue regeneration is explored. With regards to the commercialization challenges, including scalability, robustness, and performance degradation under extreme conditions, strategies to overcome these limitations and promote successful industrialization are discussed. Furthermore, the potential impacts of self-healing materials on future research directions, encompassing environmental sustainability, advanced computational techniques, integration with emerging technologies, and tailoring materials for specific applications are examined. This perspective aims to inspire interdisciplinary approaches and foster the adoption of self-healing materials in various real-life settings, ultimately contributing to the development of next-generation materials.

**Keywords:**
artificial intelligence; commercialization challenges; innovative applications; self‐healing materials; self‐healing polymers.

© 2024 The Authors. Advanced Science published by Wiley‐VCH GmbH.

SUMMARY:
Self-healing composites are innovative materials designed to autonomously repair damage and restore functionality, offering a sustainable alternative to

# Biobased self-healing functional composites and their applications

[*Journal of Materials Science: Composites*](/)
**volume 6**, Article number: 3 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1969 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x/metrics)

## Abstract

Self-healing composites are innovative materials designed to autonomously repair damage and restore functionality, offering a sustainable alternative to traditional thermosetting materials. These materials enable self-repair without external intervention, extending service life and reducing maintenance costs. Recently, bio-based self-healing composites comprising matrices and fillers derived from renewable resources such as polysaccharides (e.g., cellulose), lignin, vegetable oils, and vanillin have emerged as a promising solution to reduce dependence on non-renewable petroleum-based materials. This review delves into the advancements in bio-based self-healing composites, with a focus on systems utilizing dynamic covalent bonds (e.g., hydroxyl ester, Schiff base, disulfide bonds) and dynamic non-covalent interactions. It explores diverse self-healing mechanisms, including supramolecular chemistry, covalent bond reformation, diffusion and flow, heterogeneous systems, and shape-memory effects, as well as their synergistic combinations. The discussion spans both physical and chemical approaches, highlighting integrated physico-chemical strategies. Furthermore, the review examines state-of-the-art fabrication techniques and the broad range of applications for these materials. Future perspectives and research directions underscore the pivotal role of bio-based self-healing composites in advancing sustainable and durable solutions across multiple industries.

## Introduction

The depletion of fossil resources and rising oil prices have intensified the demand for sustainable alternatives to petroleum-based polymers. In response, bio-derived polymers have emerged as a critical focus in the development of eco-friendly and sustainable materials. Among these, bio-based self-healing materials, particularly in composite applications, stand out as a promising solution. Derived from renewable biomass sources such as vegetable oil, cellulose, chitosan, natural rubber, and proteins, these materials provide environmentally friendly alternatives that significantly reduce ecological impact.

Self-healing composites possess the ability to regain their structural integrity upon damage, e.g. self-repair mechanisms observed in living organisms. In composite materials, prolonged degradation often causes microcracks to develop, which may eventually lead to complete failure. Consequently, repair mechanisms are essential to improve the reliability and durability of these materials [[1](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR1 "
                           M. Zhu, J. Liu, L. Gan, M. Long, Research progress in bio-based self-healing materials. Eur. Polym. J. 129, 109651 (Apr. 2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2020.109651
                  
                ")]. In bio-based self-healing composites, natural polymers are combined with reinforcement materials (e.g., fibres or fillers) to enhance both mechanical properties and self-repair functionality. These composites are designed to automatically or stimuli-responsively repair internal microcracks caused by mechanical stress, thermal fluctuations, chemical exposure, or UV radiation. Traditional repair methods are often ineffective for such internal damage, but self-healing composites are capable of restoring their structural integrity on their own and significantly extending their lifespan without the need for external maintenance.

Self-healing materials are categorized into extrinsic and intrinsic. Extrinsic materials use embedded healing agents for repair but are limited by finite healing cycles. Intrinsic materials rely on reversible bonds, enabling unlimited healing triggered by stimuli like heat or light, but face challenges in balancing mechanical strength with healing efficiency. Balancing structural integrity and self-healing remains a key challenge, with strategies involving chemical, physical, or combined molecular-level approaches [[2](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR2 "C.E. Diesendruck, N.R. Sottos, J.S. Moore, S.R. White, Biomimetic Self-Healing. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 54(36), Art. no. 36, (Sep. 2015). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201500484
                  
                "), [3](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR3 "S. Utrera-Barrios, R. Verdejo, M.A. López-Manchado, M. Hernández Santana, Evolution of self-healing elastomers, from extrinsic to combined intrinsic mechanisms: a review. Mater. Horiz. 7(11), Art. no. 11, 2020, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1039/D0MH00535E
                  
                ")].

Applications of bio-based self-healing composites span multiple sectors, including aerospace, automotive, construction, sensors, energy storage devices, drug delivery and biomedical materials (Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#Fig1)). These composites offer a unique combination of mechanical strength, sustainability, and self-healing ability, making them suitable for demanding applications. In modern applications, the coupling of self-healing functionality with composites possessing electrical, optical, or chemical properties is gaining attraction.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs42252-025-00065-x/MediaObjects/42252_2025_65_Fig1_HTML.png)

Application of bio-derived self-healing materials in composites. (Adapted from [[1](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR1 "
                           M. Zhu, J. Liu, L. Gan, M. Long, Research progress in bio-based self-healing materials. Eur. Polym. J. 129, 109651 (Apr. 2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2020.109651
                  
                ")])

Since the introduction of self-healing polymers in 2007, research in this field has expanded rapidly, including advancements in self-healing composites, with over 1000 papers published on the self-healing materials by 2021 [[4](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR4 "V.I. Irzhak, I.E. Uflyand, G.I. Dzhardimalieva, Self-Healing of polymers and polymer composites. Polymers. 14, 5404 (Dec. 2022). 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/polym14245404
                  
                "), [5](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR5 "J.J. Andrew, H.N. Dhakal, Sustainable biobased composites for advanced applications: recent trends and future opportunities– A critical review. Compos. Part. C Open. Access. 7, 100220 (Mar. 2022). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcomc.2021.100220
                  
                ")]. To provide a more focused analysis, Fig. [2](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#Fig2) presents the publication trends specifically for self-healing composites.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs42252-025-00065-x/MediaObjects/42252_2025_65_Fig2_HTML.png)

Number of publications and patents on self-healing composites from 2011 to 2024, based on a Google Scholar search using “self-healing” and “composite” in title

Bio-based self-healing composites are expected to play a pivotal role in meeting the growing demand for longer-lasting, environmentally friendly materials in various industries. Despite the progress made, challenges remain, such as improving healing efficiency, mechanical performance, and scaling up production. However, ongoing research is leading to innovative solutions, driven by the demand for more sustainable, durable, and high-performance composite materials [[6](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR6 "X. Qi, C. Pan, L. Zhang, D. Yue, Bio-Based, Self-Healing, Recyclable, Reconfigurable Multifunctional Polymers with Both One-Way and Two-Way Shape Memory Properties. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 15(2), Art. no. 2, Jan. 2023, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.2c19782
                  
                ")]. To date, no comprehensive review has been reported on bio-based self-healing composites This review provides an overview of the research progress in bio-based self-healing materials and composites over the past decade, with a focus on topics relevant to the development of environmentally sustainable solutions. It provides an in-depth analysis of the latest progress in the fabrication techniques and self-healing mechanisms of these materials, as well as their potential applications.

## Biobased materials for Self-healing composite development

Various materials derived from biomass and their by-products, including vanillin, cellulose, lignin and vegetable oil have been extensively utilized in self-healing polymeric composites. The incorporation of dynamic covalent and non-covalent bonds into the biomass-derived cross-linked polymer network imparts self-healing capabilities, extending the material’s service life and minimizing waste [[7](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR7 "Y. Bei et al., Recent progress of biomass based self-healing polymers. J. Appl. Polym. Sci. 139(16), 51977 (Apr. 2022). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/app.51977
                  
                "), [8](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR8 "X. Fan et al., Dynamic Covalent Bonds Enabled Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymers Recyclability and Material Circularity. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 63(40), e202408969, (Oct. 2024), 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.202408969
                  
                ")].

### Vegetable oil

Vegetable oil contributes significantly to the development of self-healing polymers and composites by means of various innovative methods. It is used to form polymer networks by chemically modifying derivatives like epoxidized oil, which enables the creation of cross-linked structures with self-repair capabilities. Dynamic covalent bonds including Diels-Alder reactions is incorporated into these networks to allow for reversible healing under stimuli like heat. Non-covalent interactions like hydrogen bonding also help the material reassemble and heal after damage. Vegetable oil also serve as a healing agent through microencapsulation and releasing oil upon damage to fill cracks. Functionalization of vegetable oils with specific groups, such as hydroxyl or epoxy, enhances self-healing by facilitating reversible cross-linking, making these bio-based materials both sustainable and highly efficient for various applications [[7](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR7 "Y. Bei et al., Recent progress of biomass based self-healing polymers. J. Appl. Polym. Sci. 139(16), 51977 (Apr. 2022). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/app.51977
                  
                ")]. A catalyst-free vitreous elastomer was developed by Li et al., using vegetable oil (VO) in combination with dimer acid (DA) and tetrafunctional glycidyl amine epoxy resin (TGDDM). Cracks on the TD elastomer surface were healed at 200 °C, with optical microscopy measuring the self-healing performance. After 10 min, crack widths decreased by 14.0% and 44.1% for TD-1/1 and TD-1/1.2 films, respectively. After 30 min, reductions reached 29.8% and 67.8% [[9](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR9 "Y. Li, T. Liu, S. Zhang, L. Shao, M. Fei, H. Yu, J. Zhang, Catalyst-free vitrimer elastomers based on a dimer acid: robust mechanical performance, adaptability and hydrothermal recyclability. Green. Chem. 22(3), 870–881, (Jan. 2020), 
                  https://doi.org/10.1039/C9GC04080C
                  
                ")].

### Lignin

Lignin, a biopolymer sourced through plant biomass, is considered a highly potential material for the development of self-healing polymers and composites due to its functional group’s and dynamic linkages. Its inherent self-healing properties arise from the presence of hydrogen bonds and aldehyde groups within its structure, making it a viable option for long-term self-healing systems. To address issues of incompatibility with resin caused by lignin’s hyperbranched structure, lignin molecules are often modified to enhance their solubility. The production of lignin-based self-healing polymers typically entails incorporating dynamic covalent bonds or supramolecular interactions, which enable the material to repair itself when damaged. Additionally, lignin can be cross-linked with other polymers including chitosan cellulose derivatives and PVA to form self-healing hydrogels [[10](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR10 "P. Sinha Roy et al., Bio-based photo-reversible self-healing polymer designed from lignin. Green Chem. 23(24), Art. no. 24, 2021, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1039/D1GC02957F
                  
                ")]. For example, Kraft lignin (KL) was reacted with anhydride to introduce carboxyl groups, then cured with poly (ethylene glycol) diglycidyl ether (PEG-epoxy), resulting in a high-lignin-content coating (lignin-PEG) with over 47 wt% lignin. The coating displayed remarkable self-healing properties when subjected to dynamic transesterification at temperatures above 140 °C. With the use of ethylene glycol as an assisting agent, the repair efficiency of the coating reached 50%, reducing crack size from 36 μm to 18 μm [[11](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR11 "C. Hao et al., Mar., A High-Lignin‐Content, Removable, and Glycol‐Assisted Repairable Coating Based on Dynamic Covalent Bonds. ChemSusChem. 12(5), 1049–1058, 2019, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/cssc.201802615
                  
                ")].

### Cellulose

Cellulose, a plentiful material found in plants, is considered one of the most promising sustainable and tunable materials available today. Its numerous hydroxyl groups can participate in dynamic transesterification reactions. Recently, cellulose has also been utilized as an organic filler to enhance the properties of polymer materials. However, cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) offer distinct advantages over regular cellulose, including smaller sizes, a higher concentration of active functional groups, and better dispersion, all of which contribute to improved healing capabilities in materials [[12](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR12 "N. Sun et al., Jan., Development and characterization of self-healing cellulose nanofiber reinforced polyurea composite materials based on multiple dynamic interactions. Cellulose. 30(1), Art. no. 1, 2023, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10570-022-04899-7
                  
                ")]. Zhao et al. introduced an innovative vitrimer composite (VC) comprising a polycarbonate matrix reinforced with natural cellulose paper. The self-healing performance of the VC film was evaluated by monitoring variations in the size of scratches at 160 °C under a polarizing microscope. Scratches created with a blade demonstrated a minimum healing efficiency of 75% within 10 s. This rapid repair process was driven by hydroxyl-carbonate exchange reactions at elevated temperatures and enabled the formation of reversible covalent bonds [[13](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR13 "W. Zhao et al., Oct., Vitrimer-Cellulose Paper Composites: A New Class of Strong, Smart, Green, and Sustainable Materials. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 11(39), 36090–36099, 2019, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.9b11991
                  
                ")].

### Vanillin

Vanillin, a natural aldehyde found abundantly in vanilla and eucalyptus leaves, particularly in the vanillin clip and is valued for its high reactivity due to its phenolic hydroxyl and aldehyde groups. This makes it a key precursor in the production of valuable monomers. Additionally, vanillin is used to synthesize materials with varying mechanical properties, including self-healing materials [[14](#ref-CR14 "M.A. Rashid, S. Zhu, L. Zhang, K. Jin, W. Liu, High-performance and fully recyclable epoxy resins cured by imine-containing hardeners derived from Vanillin and syringaldehyde. Eur. Polym. J. 187, 111878 (Apr. 2023). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2023.111878
                  
                "),[15](#ref-CR15 "M.A. Rashid, M.Y. Ali, M.A. Islam, M.A. Kafi, Investigating the structure-performance correlation of amines based recyclable Vanillin epoxy thermosets. Ind. Crops Prod. 224, 120303 (Feb. 2025). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indcrop.2024.120303
                  
                "),[16](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR16 "H. Memon et al., Vanillin-Based epoxy vitrimer with high performance and Closed-Loop recyclability. Macromolecules. 53(2), 621–630 (Jan. 2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.macromol.9b02006
                  
                ")]. Vanillin also formed Schiff bases (imine bonds) through the reaction of its aldehyde group with amines. Imine bonds are dynamic covalent bonds, which break and reform and facilitating self-healing when the material is damaged. This type of chemistry allows the polymer to autonomously repair itself when exposed to appropriate conditions, such as heat or light. Nowadays, vanillin also used as a building block for vitrimers. Vitrimers are a class of polymers that exhibit both the malleability of thermoplastics and the mechanical stability of thermosets due to dynamic covalent networks [[17](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR17 "
                           K. Wei, Y. Wu, X. Cao, X. Yang, B. Tang, L. Deng, Preparation and characterization of vanillin-based self-healing polyurethane-modified asphalts based on acylhydrazone chemistry. Eur. Polym. J. 211, 113010 (May 2024). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2024.113010
                  
                "), [18](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR18 "M.A. Rashid, M.N. Hasan, M.A.R. Dayan, M.S. Ibna Jamal, M.K. Patoary, A critical review of sustainable Vanillin-modified vitrimers: synthesis, challenge and prospects. Reactions. 4(1), 66–91 (Jan. 2023). 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/reactions4010003
                  
                ")].

### Polysaccharide

A natural polysaccharide is a large, complex carbohydrate composed of long chains of monosaccharides linked together through glycosidic bonds. These polysaccharides are naturally occurring and are produced by living organisms such as plants, animals, bacteria, and fungi. They play a range of biological roles, such as storing energy, providing structural support, and facilitating cellular communication. Natural polysaccharides like chitosan and hyaluronic acid can undergo chemical modifications to create self-healing hydrogels based on acylhydrazone and imine system [[19](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR19 "H. Liu, X. Sui, H. Xu, L. Zhang, Y. Zhong, Z. Mao, Self-Healing polysaccharide hydrogel based on dynamic covalent enamine bonds. Macromol. Mater. Eng. 301 (2016). 6, Art. 6, Jun. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/mame.201600042
                  
                ")]. Zhang and colleagues developed dynamic, self-repairing hydrogels based on chitosan (CTS) through the formation of a Schiff base. A dynamic covalent bond was formed between the amine groups of chitosan (CTS) and the benzaldehyde groups at the termini of bifunctional poly (ethylene glycol) (DF-PEG). The reversible cleavage and reformation of the imine bonds endowed the hydrogel with remarkable self-healing capabilities [[20](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR20 "Y. Zhang, L. Tao, S. Li, Y. Wei, Synthesis of Multiresponsive and Dynamic Chitosan-Based Hydrogels for Controlled Release of Bioactive Molecules. Biomacromolecules. 12(8), 2894–2901, (Aug. 2011), 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/bm200423f
                  
                ")]. Also, in another study, injectable self-healing hydrogels composed of natural polysaccharides, designed to emulate the extracellular matrix and enhance cellular proliferation, represent promising materials for wound healing applications. In this study, a new hydrogel was developed using carboxymethyl chitosan and aldehyde-functionalized sodium alginate, crosslinked through a Schiff base reaction [[21](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR21 "H. Xuan, S. Wu, S. Fei, B. Li, Y. Yang, H. Yuan, Injectable nanofiber-polysaccharide self-healing hydrogels for wound healing. Mater. Sci. Eng. C 128, 112264 (Sep. 2021). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2021.112264
                  
                ")].

## Self-Healing mechanisms in composites

Self-healing materials are primarily classified into two types intrinsic and extrinsic based on their healing mechanisms [[22](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR22 "X. He, K. Yu, Additive manufacturing of self-healing polymers and composites. Recent Advances in Smart Self-Healing Polymers and Composites, Elsevier, 2022, 433–456. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-823472-3.00010-2
                  
                ")]. Extrinsic self-healing materials incorporate healing agents embedded within a matrix, typically organized as capsules or vascular networks. When damage occurs, these capsules or networks rupture, and release the healing agents that then repair and seal the affected areas. While extrinsic materials allow for rapid and efficient self-healing over large surfaces, their healing capability is limited by the finite number of available healing cycles [[23](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR23 "K. Choi, A. Noh, J. Kim, P.H. Hong, M.J. Ko, S.W. Hong, Properties and Applications of Self-Healing Polymeric Materials: A Review. Polymers. 15(22), Art. no. 22, (Nov. 2023), 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/polym15224408
                  
                ")]. This limitation arises because their effectiveness in addressing subsequent damage at the same site is hindered by the depletion of the healing agent [[22](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR22 "X. He, K. Yu, Additive manufacturing of self-healing polymers and composites. Recent Advances in Smart Self-Healing Polymers and Composites, Elsevier, 2022, 433–456. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-823472-3.00010-2
                  
                ")]. In contrast, intrinsic self-healing materials employ unique internal reversible reactions and bonding mechanisms to autonomously repair damage, allowing them to restore structural integrity without the need for external healing agents and enabling unlimited healing cycles [[2](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR2 "C.E. Diesendruck, N.R. Sottos, J.S. Moore, S.R. White, Biomimetic Self-Healing. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 54(36), Art. no. 36, (Sep. 2015). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201500484
                  
                ")]. In another words, through this mechanism, dynamic bonding has been rap-idly advanced, allowing for repeated healing without the worry of depleting the healing agent. This reversible mechanism is triggered by damage occurred from external stimuli including water, heat or light. Although intrinsic self-healing mechanisms offer the advantage of unlimited healing cycles, these materials face a significant challenge. Typically, materials with high mechanical strength exhibit limited chain mobility and elevated glass transition temperatures, which hinder the self-healing process. Consequently, achieving both optimal self-healing and mechanical performance simultaneously is often difficult [[24](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR24 "H. Feng, F. Yu, Y. Guo, W. Wang, L. Xiao, Y. Liu, An exceptional strength, self-healing and recyclability polyurethane elastomers via multiple hydrogen bonds optimization strategy. Appl. Surf. Sci. 655, 159560 (May 2024). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.159560
                  
                ")]. Enhancing mechanical strength requires increasing the interactions between polymer chains, but this leads to restricted chain mobility and, as a result, diminished self-healing capabilities [[3](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR3 "S. Utrera-Barrios, R. Verdejo, M.A. López-Manchado, M. Hernández Santana, Evolution of self-healing elastomers, from extrinsic to combined intrinsic mechanisms: a review. Mater. Horiz. 7(11), Art. no. 11, 2020, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1039/D0MH00535E
                  
                ")]. Overall, the main strategies for developing self-healing polymers rely on molecular-level processes, which can be chemical, physical, or a combination of both. Several approaches for introducing self-healing capabilities into materials are depicted in Fig. [3](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#Fig3) [[25](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR25 "S. Wang, M.W. Urban, Self-healing polymers. Nat. Rev. Mater. 5(8), Art. no. 8, Jun. 2020, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41578-020-0202-4
                  
                ")].

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs42252-025-00065-x/MediaObjects/42252_2025_65_Fig3_HTML.png)

Designing approached for self-healing bio-based composites

### Physical approaches

Various physical processes, such as shape-memory effects, the incorporation of phase-separated morphologies, the interdiffusion of polymer chains, and the integration of active nanopar-ticles within a polymer matrix, can be employed to achieve self-healing properties through physical mechanisms [[25](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR25 "S. Wang, M.W. Urban, Self-healing polymers. Nat. Rev. Mater. 5(8), Art. no. 8, Jun. 2020, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41578-020-0202-4
                  
                ")]. Shape memory, one of the most extensively studied categories, is not restricted to any specific polymer chemistry or microstructure; instead, it emerges from a combination of programming, molecular architecture, reversible changes in mobility, and conformational entropy. For a polymer to exhibit shape-memory properties, two key structural components are required: (I) permanent junctions or networks that form a three-dimensional structure to retain the permanent shape and prevent chain slippage during deformation, and (II) reversible molecular segments that fix or release the temporary shape in response to external stimuli [[26](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR26 "C.C. Hornat, M.W. Urban, Shape memory effects in self-healing polymers. Prog Polym. Sci. 102, 101208 (Mar. 2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.progpolymsci.2020.101208
                  
                ")]. The shape memory influence in synthetic polymers holds great potential when carefully de-signed. These polymers can retain a permanent shape (Fig. [4](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#Fig4)) be deformed into a temporary one, and then revert to their original form when stimulated by external or internal stimuli such as deformation, light and heat [[25](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR25 "S. Wang, M.W. Urban, Self-healing polymers. Nat. Rev. Mater. 5(8), Art. no. 8, Jun. 2020, 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41578-020-0202-4
                  
                ")], magnetism, and electricity [[27](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR27 "K.Y. Shen, X.J. Wang, H.J. Chen, Advances in light-activated shape memory polymer: A brief review. Mater. Today Commun. 41, 110247 (Dec. 2024). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.110247
                  
                ")]. This response which is governed by entropy, involves conformational changes or chain contractions that enables mass flow and self-healing.

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs42252-025-00065-x/MediaObjects/42252_2025_65_Fig4_HTML.jpg)

Self-healing through shape-memory mechanism

For example, in semi-crystalline networks, crystallites function as temporary physical cross-links that maintain deformations below the material’s melting point. Upon heating above the melting or glass transition point [[28](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR28 "H. Zhang, Y. Zhao, Polymers with dual Light-Triggered functions of shape memory and healing using gold nanoparticles. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 5 (2013). 24, Art. 24, Dec. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1021/am404087q
                  
                ")], the material returns to its original shape through rubber elasticity, with permanent covalent cross-links guiding the net-work chains back to their most stable that is their high-entropy state.

### Chemical approaches (Supramolecular-chemistry)

#### Guest-host chemistry

Host–guest interaction, a type of non-covalent interaction based on a lock-and-key principle, is applied in self-healing biopolymers by embedding guest molecules or nanoparticles within a host polymer matrix. These guest components interact non-covalently with the host, facilitating repair [[29](/articles/10.1186/s42252-025-00065-x#ref-CR29 "K. Buaksuntear, P. Limarun, S. Suethao, W. Smitthipong, Non-Covalent interaction on the Self-Healing of mechanical properties in supramolecular polymers. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 23 (2022). 13, Art. 13, Jun. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms23136902
                  
                ")]. These reversible interactions such as hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic forces, or coordination compl

Sustainability of self-healing polymers: A holistic perspective towards circularity in polymer networks
=======================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Kenneth Cerdan a b 1, Marlies Thys c d 1, Aleix Costa Cornellà d 1, Fatma Demir d e, Sophie Norvez f, Richard Vendamme c, Niko Van den Brande d, Peter Van Puyvelde g, Joost Brancart d

Abstract
--------

Permanent [polymer networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-network "Learn more about polymer networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") present an important sustainability challenge. Irreversible covalent crosslinks impart these materials excellent mechanical properties, thermal and chemical resistance, yet also render them difficult to repair and to recycle. Self-healing mechanisms can extend the lifetime of [thermosets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermosets "Learn more about thermosets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [elastomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/elastomer "Learn more about elastomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), improving their durability and making their lifecycle more sustainable. In addition to the lifetime extension, this paper reviews the sustainability of self-healing polymers from a holistic point of view. The entire lifecycle of self-healing polymers is critically assessed with reference to the green chemistry principles and sustainable development. The relation between the self-healing chemistries and the sustainability aspects of each of the phases of the lifecycle are discussed, starting from the [feedstocks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/feedstock "Learn more about feedstocks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), monomer functionalisation and polymer synthesis, to processing and manufacturing as well as end-of-life considerations, _i.e._ recycling or (bio)degradation. The review provides a toolbox for the development of more sustainable [thermosets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thermoset-plastics "Learn more about thermosets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [elastomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/elastomer "Learn more about elastomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and their composites. It is of utmost importance to consider the entire lifecycle of self-healing materials, derived products and – by extension – any material or product. The self-healing ability and often related recyclability should primarily reduce the amount of new materials that are necessary to fulfill societal needs, by extending the lifetime of products and maximizing reprocessing into new products. Increasing healing efficiency and the number of healing cycles improves the overall environmental impact relative to the extended service lifetime. Renewable resources derived from biomass, recycling processes or waste streams should be the first choice to create new self-healing polymers. Finally, biodegradability can be considered as a complementary end-of-life scenario upon accidental loss of self-healing polymer to the environment, provided that the biodegradation does not start under the prospected use conditions of the self-healing polymers and products, but can be postponed until contact with stimuli present in the environment.

1. Introduction
---------------

Already in the 1840′s, [natural rubbers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/rubber "Learn more about natural rubbers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were being vulcanized and other [natural polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/natural-polymer "Learn more about natural polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as cellulose were processed to make the first man-made polymers. Just before the 20th century, the first artificial polymers were created from milk casein and formaldehyde, followed by the conception of the first [thermoset](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermosets "Learn more about thermoset from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by Leo Baekeland, Bakelite. Over the course of more than a century, natural and artificial polymers have contributed considerably to the improvement of our everyday lives. However, it has also been shown that the deficient resource management and end-of-life waste build-up of these polymers pose tremendous risks for the health of humankind and its environment. The risks that their synthetic chemistries entail (high energy demand, use of organic solvents…), as well as physicochemical stability and high-volume output of the final polymer products, have become a threat to life on Earth, menacing wildlife and human health as a consequence of the massive amount of waste formation. Plastic waste can be found throughout oceans, rivers, soils, and even in freshwater streams used for human consumption in the shape of [microplastics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/microplastics "Learn more about microplastics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). It is estimated that, following the current trends, about 12,000 Mt of plastic waste will be discarded in landfills or the natural environment by 2025, while nowadays only an estimated amount of 9 % of the total manufactured plastics are being recycled [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0001). The proportion diminishes markedly to less than 1 % for plastic recycled more than once. While [thermoplastics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermoplastics "Learn more about thermoplastics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may be recycled by way of (thermo)mechanical methods, thermosets and [elastomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/elastomer "Learn more about elastomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are crosslinked by irreversible bonds cannot be reprocessed. Permanent [covalent bonds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/covalent-bond "Learn more about covalent bonds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") impart superior mechanical properties, thermal and chemical stability to crosslinked polymers, compared to their linear or physically crosslinked analogues, while rendering them challenging to recycle.

The ubiquitous societal demand towards a sustainable economy belongs to some of present-day government agendas. To face this challenge, it is required to devise and set the ground rules that must be integrated in future business mindset. The most representative and accepted sustainability assessment benchmark corresponds the 12 principles of Green Chemistry introduced by Anastas and Eghbali in 2010 [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0002). These principles serve as the basis to establish the main prerequisites to design sustainable products and operations. The depletion of petroleum resources must be accompanied by the implementation of these guidelines. The first principle pertains the _prevention of waste formation_. In the scope of this review, this involves every step of the material's life cycle. The second principle is to _promote atom efficiency_ in synthetic methods, which contributes to waste prevention by minimizing side products and emissions. The third principle is to _avoid the use of hazardous or toxic substances_ and methodologies. _Preservation of the efficacy of the employed chemicals_ while improving their safety and to _avoid the use of_ _[auxiliary](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/auxiliaries "Learn more about auxiliary from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _substances_ such as solvents or additives are the next principles, while _maximizing the energy efficiency of the process_. If auxiliary substances would be necessary, they should be innocuous. The _use of renewable resources_ should be enhanced, the _use of derivatives should be minimized_ and _catalysts should be employed as selective as possible_ to perform chemical reactions. _Promoting the degradability_ of the prepared products marks the tenth principle. The final two principles are the design of _analytical methods to in-situ monitor the formation of pollutants_ and _to minimize accident risks_ (explosions, fires, leakages) by using safer chemicals.

Anastas and Eghbali placed waste prevention at the top of the list. Firstly, waste prevention leads to an improved atom economy throughout the entire lifecycle of the material and no matter is lost to the environment. Secondly, the energy stored in the material can be recovered and reused if waste is collected and recycled. No new resources need to be extracted, as the material remains in the loop. Inspired by Nature, scientists came up with the idea that an effective route towards waste prevention can be realized through self-healing of damage in synthetic materials, mimicking natural organisms. Many natural materials can regenerate when injured, restoring their functionality to that previous to damage. Wound sealing, bone healing or even DNA repair are some examples of the outstanding healing performance of biological systems [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0003). The application of this concept to synthetic materials was adopted for the first time during the 80′s by means of intermolecular diffusion of polymer chains when heated above _T g_[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0004). This finding has prompted the conceptualization of novel advanced engineered materials, so-called self-healing materials, that can repair their structure upon external damage, avoiding catastrophic failure. It is evidenced that the concept of self-healing materials has the potential to contribute greatly towards the sustainability of all classes of materials by limiting waste formation and avoiding the need to produce new products. However, not all that glitters is gold. Despite the promising outcomes, the sometimes devaluated “green” or “eco-friendly” terms have blindsided the critical perspective of whether a material or procedure is truly sustainable or not, disregarding other fundamental criteria such as energy efficiency, carbon footprint or employment of toxic reactants. This also applies to self-healing polymers, as their chemical composition, production or healing triggers might result in environmentally more pernicious solutions than their classic plastic counterparts. Therefore, this review will also consider other aspects from the Green Chemistry principles of Anastas and Eghbali, such as the use of environmentally taxing chemicals and auxiliary substances, the [feedstocks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/feedstock "Learn more about feedstocks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and their derivatives, the energy consumption during synthesis, the processing and manufacturing and end-of-life considerations such as the recyclability and degradability.

Complementary to the Green Chemistry principles and more from an engineering perspective, circular economy models have been developed that involve an increasing number of R-words. Already several decades ago, we learned about Reduce, Reuse and Recycle. As the efforts of consumers and producers to limit waste production were and are still severely lacking, these models have been expanded with more R-words [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0005). However, these merely provide examples and ultimately boil down to the original three. Refuse is the customer's strongest signal to Reduce the consumption and, ultimately, the production of obsolete products. Redesign and Rethink are methods to Reduce waste formation and to improve Reusability and Recyclability. Remanufacture is a subsection of Recycling that also focuses on the Reuse of components into new products, without needing to completely Recycle the materials. Renew and Repair are practical examples how to extend the lifecycle of certain products and to enhance Reusability. Finally, it is possible to Recover (part of) the energy invested in the materials through incineration. This is the least desirable form of Recycling. Ultimately, the chances for success of any of these R-words, however many are proposed, depend on the willingness of consumers, producers and policy makers to put these R-words into general practice. The lack of waste management resources and policies demands a hasty remedy to both academia and industry to find potential solutions that might mitigate this sharply rising problem. The formulation and manufacturing of novel polymeric materials that are recyclable, biodegradable or bio-sourced are some of the proposed alternatives, despite still not generally practiced due to financial and scale constraints.

Self-healing materials have repair mechanisms built into their [material structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/materials-structure "Learn more about material structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that enable them to “heal” after damage and recover their functional properties, similar to biological systems. They primarily target lifetime extension and hence reuse of the product or component. For over three decades, a broad range of different healing strategies and related self-healing formulations have been proposed in scientific literature. Two major classifications have been adopted based on their healing mechanism. Extrinsic self-healing materials are based on the incorporation of capsules, hollow fibres or vascular networks to store a healing agent within a [polymer matrix](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-matrix "Learn more about polymer matrix from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Upon mechanical damage, the healing agent leaks out of the reservoir to fill the damage volume and cure to recover the mechanical properties of the material. Intrinsic self-healing materials owe their properties to the presence of physicochemical interactions or dynamic chemical bonds in the [polymer structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-structure "Learn more about polymer structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Upon damage, these bonds are broken in a reversible fashion and can be reformed at the damage surfaces to reconnect the damaged parts. The mechanism resembles the classic intermolecular diffusion approach, with the added value of providing bond exchange upon randomization at the crack location. In general, extrinsic self-healing systems can autonomously repair damage as the mechanical force itself triggers the healing action upon rupture of the reservoir containing the liquid healing agent, while intrinsic healing mechanisms often need to be submitted to an external stimulus, such as heat or light, to activate the healing mechanism. In certain instances, the physicochemical interactions or dynamic covalent bonds can be reversibly broken by the damaging force and possess sufficient mobility and reactivity to autonomously reform and heal the damage, without the need for external intervention, but often at the expense of the mechanical properties.

Self-healing materials are a direct solution to the first Green Chemistry principle, since waste is prevented by extending the lifetime through damage healing and reuse, and thus avoiding the need for disposal of damaged products and the production of replacement products ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#fig0001)). However, current self-healing materials and their synthetic methods do not necessarily follow the other Green Chemistry principles. For example, some self-healing polymers might be based on biomass-derived resources, but the healing step required to restore the material properties can be highly energy demanding. Hence, there exists room for the development of more sustainable alternative routes towards novel self-healing materials formulations, or to appraise how much can non-sustainable methods be elongated to compensate other limitations associated to the actual greener approaches. The aim of this review is to provide scientists from both industry and academy with a toolbox for a sustainable development of future self-healing materials, and to do an in-depth analysis of how self-healing relates to sustainability and how it can be improved. In the framework of this study, extra attention will be paid to intrinsic systems since these are considered the group with a larger optimization window to obtain durable and efficient healable polymeric systems, focusing on the different dynamic chemistries in charge of their stimuli-responsiveness.

![Image 12: Fig 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0079670024000339-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (983KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0079670024000339-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (983KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0079670024000339-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. The circularity of self-healing polymers: The 12 green chemistry principles (left) are linked to each of the phases of the life cycle (right) and to the circularity principles (middle). The highlights (bottom) summarize the contributions of the self-healing chemistries and mechanisms to their overall sustainability.

Many types of self-healing materials that possess the ability to repair damage, autonomously or under the application of an external or internal stimulus, have been developed in the past decades in view of improving many aspects of the sustainability of these [material types](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/materials-class "Learn more about material types from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In current literature, the service lifetime extension by healing incurred damage has been the major aspect considered in the sustainability assessment of this recent class of smart materials. In this literature review, the authors thoroughly assess the efforts performed to improve the sustainability of all phases from the cradle to the grave of self-healing polymers. Different classes and types of self-healing polymers are compared to their traditional, non-recyclable and non-self-healing alternatives. Haines-Gadd et al. raised the discussion whether self-healing materials would lead to “immortal” products or would pose a risk to a circular economy [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0006). They found that despite the intensity of research efforts in the materials science community in the development of self-healing materials, there was a lack of debate regarding the implications of product lifetime extension on the implementation of self-healing products in a circular economy. So far, there have been no life cycle assessments (LCA) on self-healing polymers, while this is already much more mature for [cementitious](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/cementitious "Learn more about cementitious from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") self-healing materials. Bernardo et al. compared the life cycles of concretes based on a self-healing geopolymer with ordinary [Portland cement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/portland-cement "Learn more about Portland cement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (OPC) [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0007). The self-healing geopolymer concrete was much more prone to microcrack formation, yet able to extend the lifetime by healing these microcracks using microcapsules filled with a healing agent. The performance in global warming potential was found to be better for the self-healing geopolymer concrete, but worse in other environmental impact categories than OPC concrete. The boundaries of the LCA were limited from cradle to gate, not considering the effect of the self-healing properties of the useful lifetime of the concrete in their application. Another cradle-to-gate LCA was performed on self-healing concrete by the De Belie group, comparing different superabsorbent polymers [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0008). They estimated that considering 100 % healing efficiency and accounting for the required replacements over a course of 100 years, the CML-IA impacts of the self-healing concretes remain lower than traditional concrete.

Most life cycle assessments (LCA), life cycle costings (LCC) and techno-economical assessments (TEA) limit the boundaries of the assessment from gate to gate, from cradle to gate or at most from cradle to grave, yet even in the latter not accounting for the use phase and potential implications of lifetime extension. Ramesh et al. came to these conclusions after reviewing LCA and TEA on polymers, often comparing fossil fuel-based polymers with bio-based alternatives [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079670024000339#bib0009). Bernardo et al. concluded that many of the LCA on polymers and [polymer composites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-composite "Learn more about polymer composites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were consistent and found that global warming

enriched_points.expanded_content: Self-healing polymers are transforming various industries by providing materials that autonomously repair themselves, thus enhancing durability and functionality, leading to innovative applications in fields like aerospace and electronics.

These materials reduce waste and promote resource conservation by extending product lifespans, aligning with global sustainability goals and utilizing bio-based resources to lessen environmental impact.
Ongoing research is essential to overcome current challenges in scalability, efficiency, and performance, ensuring the development of practical self-healing polymers for widespread industrial use.
